issues.name: Passkey (WebAuthn/FIDO2) authentication — register, login, manage
issues.description:
Add passkeys (WebAuthn/FIDO2) as a first-class authentication method in authkit, alongside password, OIDC, and SIWS. Passkeys are phishing-resistant, usernameless-capable credentials bound to the relying party (RP) domain. A user can register one or more passkeys and authenticate with them; a successful login mints the SAME access/refresh session as the password path (and honors the optional `org` body param).

LIBRARY: github.com/go-webauthn/webauthn for ceremony options + attestation/assertion verification. authkit owns storage, ephemeral challenge handling, session minting, routing, policy.

RP CONFIG (host-provided, on core.Config): RPID (registrable domain), RPDisplayName, allowed Origins. Derive defaults from BaseURL/Issuer; validate RPID is a registrable suffix of each origin.

CEREMONIES (begin -> finish; challenge state in the EphemeralStore, same pattern as SIWS challenges + reset tokens, short-TTL single-use): REGISTRATION (AUTH'd user) begin->CreationOptions (challenge, RP, per-user handle, excludeCredentials, residentKey=preferred) + finish (verify attestation, store credential). AUTHENTICATION (login) begin->RequestOptions supporting BOTH discoverable/usernameless AND username-scoped (prefer discoverable) + finish (verify assertion, sign-count clone detection, update sign_count/last_used, mint session).

STORAGE: new profiles.user_passkeys (id uuidv7, user_id fk, credential_id bytea UNIQUE, public_key bytea, sign_count bigint, aaguid bytea, transports text[], attestation_fmt text, label, created_at, last_used_at, deleted_at). A per-user random user_handle (NOT the user id) maps handle->user for usernameless login.

SECURITY: RPID/origin phishing-resistance (library-enforced); sign-count regression -> reject (clone); single-use short-TTL challenges; anti-enumeration on username-scoped login begin; rate-limit begin+finish; live-user ban/deleted gate on login.

MIGRATION PACKAGING (do it right): add profiles.user_passkeys as a NEW NUMBERED migration (002_user_passkeys.up.sql), NOT appended to the consolidated 001 file — migratekit is name-tracked and won't re-apply 001 to DBs that already recorded it, so tables added to 001 never reach existing deployments (the org_access_tokens gotcha). A new numbered file IS applied to existing DBs.

ROUTES (new RouteGroup RoutePasskeys): POST /passkeys/register/begin (AUTH), POST /passkeys/register/finish (AUTH), POST /passkeys/login/begin (PUBLIC), POST /passkeys/login/finish (PUBLIC), GET /passkeys (AUTH; metadata only), DELETE /passkeys/{id} (AUTH), PATCH /passkeys/{id} rename (AUTH, optional).

NON-GOALS: enterprise/attestation-conveyance policy (accept 'none'); MDS metadata validation; account recovery when all passkeys are lost (rely on existing password/email recovery).
tasks: [ ] Add go-webauthn dep; WebAuthn RP config on core.Config (RPID, RPDisplayName, Origins) + BaseURL-derived defaults + validation (RPID a registrable suffix of each origin).
[ ] NEW numbered migration 002_user_passkeys.up.sql: profiles.user_passkeys + indexes (unique credential_id, index user_id). Do NOT append to 001.
[ ] Storage: CRUD for user_passkeys (create/list-by-user/get-by-credential-id/update-sign-count+last-used/soft-delete) + per-user user_handle generation & handle->user lookup.
[ ] Registration ceremony: begin (CreationOptions, excludeCredentials, ephemeral single-use challenge) + finish (verify attestation, persist). AUTH-gated.

[ ] Authentication ceremony: begin (discoverable + username-scoped, anti-enumeration) + finish (verify assertion, sign-count clone-check, update sign_count/last_used, mint access+refresh honoring `org`, live-user gate).
[ ] Management routes: GET /passkeys (metadata only), DELETE /passkeys/{id}, optional PATCH rename.
[ ] RouteGroup RoutePasskeys + registration; challenge state via EphemeralStore (single-use, short TTL) like SIWS.
[ ] Rate-limit buckets for register/login begin+finish; anti-enumeration on username-scoped login begin.
[ ] Tests: full register + login ceremonies via a software-authenticator fixture; sign-count regression rejection; usernameless login; list/delete; anti-enumeration; rate limits.
[ ] Docs: api-endpoints.md + README passkey section (RP config, ceremony flow, frontend navigator.credentials notes, security model, recovery out-of-scope).
[ ] Version bump + publish; consumer notes (host mounts RoutePasskeys + sets RP config; frontend integrates the WebAuthn JS ceremonies).

issues.name: Resource-scoped Organization Access Tokens
issues.description:
Extend AuthKit Organization Access Tokens from `credential -> org -> permissions` to `credential -> org -> resource scope -> permissions`, while preserving the existing opaque-token security model. The core rule is: permissions describe WHAT the OAT may do; resource scopes describe WHERE it may do it.

Motivation: OpenRails needs OATs that can be tenant-wide (`tenant=tensorhub, payer=*`) or payer-scoped (`tenant=tensorhub, payer=cozy-art`) without making AuthKit infrastructure-multi-tenant and without encoding resource ids into permission strings. The same primitive should work for any embedding host: AuthKit stores and resolves host-defined resource scopes opaquely, while the host enforces their meaning.

Current OAT model:
- Token is opaque: `<prefix>oat_<key_id>_<secret>`.
- AuthKit stores org ownership, permissions, expiry/revocation, and last-used state.
- `ResolveOrgAccessToken` returns org slug + permissions.

Target OAT model:
- Token remains opaque and revocable.
- AuthKit stores zero or more resource-scope grants alongside the token.
- `ResolveOrgAccessToken` returns org slug + permissions + resource scopes.
- Existing tokens with no resource scopes keep working as org-wide tokens unless a host opts into requiring scopes.

Example OpenRails interpretation:
- tenant-wide service OAT: resources=[{kind:"openrails.tenant", id:"tensorhub"}], permissions=["openrails:admin"]
- payer-scoped spend OAT: resources=[{kind:"openrails.tenant", id:"tensorhub"}, {kind:"openrails.payer_org", id:"cozy-art"}], permissions=["openrails:credits:spend"]

AuthKit must not interpret OpenRails resource kinds. Resource kind/id strings are host-defined and opaque to AuthKit, like app-defined permission strings. AuthKit owns storage, mint/list/resolve/revoke APIs, no-escalation checks where applicable, and contract shape. Host apps own semantic enforcement.
tasks: [x] Define the OAT resource-scope contract: stable type names (`OrgAccessTokenResource{Kind, ID}`), exact-match storage semantics, no wildcard-by-default except explicit host-provided ids such as `*`, and no AuthKit interpretation of host resource kinds.

[x] Add a NEW numbered Postgres migration, not a 001 edit: create `profiles.org_access_token_resources` keyed by OAT id, with `kind`, `resource_id`, timestamps, uniqueness on `(token_id, kind, resource_id)`, and an index for token lookup.

[x] Extend core types: add `Resources []OrgAccessTokenResource` to `OrgAccessToken`; add `ResolvedOrgAccessToken` with org slug, permissions, resources, token id, and key id metadata without breaking existing callers.
[x] Add mint options for resources without changing the opaque token format: `MintOrgAccessTokenWithOptions`; keep the existing `MintOrgAccessToken` wrapper for compatibility.
[x] Update `ListOrgAccessTokens` to include resource-scope metadata, never secrets.

[x] Update `ResolveOrgAccessToken` flow to load resources in the same resolution path as permissions and return them through `ResolveOrgAccessTokenWithResources`. Expiry, revocation, org-deleted checks, last_used_at update, and constant-time secret comparison remain unchanged.

[x] Decide and implement compatibility behavior: existing `ResolveOrgAccessToken(ctx, keyID, secret) -> (org, permissions, err)` stays as a wrapper, while the new resource-aware resolver returns resources; docs note resource-aware hosts must use the new resolver / middleware claims.
[x] Add optional HTTP support on POST `/orgs/{org}/access-tokens`: accept `resources` as an array of `{kind,id}`; validate shape and length only. GET returns resources. DELETE/revoke is unchanged.

[x] Preserve no-escalation for permissions. Resource-scope escalation is host-defined, so AuthKit accepts an optional host `ResourceScopeAuthorizer` callback for HTTP minting; without one, route-level creation allows resources only for callers already allowed to manage OATs for the org.

[x] Tests: mint/list/resolve tokens with no resources, one resource, multiple resources, duplicate resource rejection, revoke/expiry still enforced, resource metadata never includes secrets, existing API wrappers continue to work.
[x] Docs: update README and `agents/api-endpoints.md` with resource-scoped OAT semantics, examples, security guidance, and the rule `permissions say what; resources say where`.

[x] OpenRails follow-up issue: added OpenRails issue 310 to consume resource-aware OAT resolution for `openrails.tenant` and optional `openrails.payer_org` scopes, with the PayerOrgID/invoker terminology issue tracked separately as 309.

issues.name: Hard-cut AuthKit org vocabulary to tenants, memberships, and service tokens
issues.description:
Replace AuthKit's `org` model with industry-standard tenant/account vocabulary and remove all legacy organization naming in one hard cut.

## Target terminology

- AuthKit instance / realm: one deployed AuthKit database + issuer boundary. Do not call this a tenant.
- tenant: the customer/account/workspace boundary inside an AuthKit instance. This is the old org concept.
- native user: a login-capable user registered directly in this AuthKit instance.
- tenant membership: the association between a native user and a tenant, with exactly one tenant role unless a later issue explicitly changes that rule.
- tenant role: a tenant-scoped role containing permissions/scopes.
- tenant issuer: an OIDC issuer controlled by a tenant and trusted to assert delegated users.
- delegated user / federated user: an external principal identified by OIDC `iss` + `sub`, not a native login-capable AuthKit user.
- service token: an opaque, revocable, tenant-owned server-to-server credential. This replaces org access token terminology.

## Hard-cut policy

No compatibility aliases, no dual routes, no deprecated field fallback, and no old database views. Remove `org`, `organization`, `owner org`, `operator org`, `admin tenant`, and `platform tenant` naming from AuthKit's public API, Go API, migrations, docs, route names, CLI, tests, and examples.

## Industry/OIDC alignment

Use OIDC names for federation (`issuer`, `subject`, `audience`, `jwks_uri`, `kid`, `claims`) and SaaS identity names for the account boundary (`tenant`, `membership`, `role`, `service token`). Avoid overloading OIDC `tenant` to mean the deployed AuthKit instance; call that an AuthKit instance or realm.

## Concrete instance shapes from the design

- OpenRails embedded AuthKit: `tenants` has the sole bootstrapped tenant such as `doujins-hentai0`; `users` is empty because native-user registration is closed; `delegated_users` references external Doujins/Hentai0 users via OIDC `iss` + `sub`.
- Doujins/Hentai0 AuthKit: `users` contains the real local users; `tenants` can be empty; `delegated_users` can be empty.
- Tensorhub AuthKit: `tenants` contains customer accounts such as `cozy-art`; `users` contains native Tensorhub users; `tenant_users` links users to tenants with one role; `tenant_roles` defines permissions; `delegated_users` can reference users whose source of truth lives in a tenant system.

## Naming rule

Eliminate `org` / `organization` as AuthKit terminology. The durable names are `tenant`, `tenant_user`, `tenant_role`, `tenant_issuer`, `delegated_user`, and `service_token`.
tasks: [ ] Inventory all `org`, `organization`, `org_user`, `org_role`, `org_access_token`, `operator_org`, and related naming across schema, migrations, Go types, routes, docs, examples, tests, and consumer-facing errors.

[ ] Hard-cut all AuthKit database/API names to tenant vocabulary only: prefer concrete table/type names `tenants`, `tenant_users`, `tenant_roles`, `tenant_issuers`, `delegated_users`, and `service_tokens`; do not keep `org`/`organization` in public or internal AuthKit names.

[ ] Produce and apply a hard-cut rename map: `orgs -> tenants`, `org_users -> tenant_users`, `org_roles -> tenant_roles`, `org_access_tokens -> service_tokens` or `tenant_service_tokens`, and `Org*` Go/API types to `Tenant*` / `TenantUser*` / `ServiceToken*` names.

[ ] Add new numbered migrations that rename tables, columns, indexes, constraints, and enum/comment text where the old `org` concept means tenant/account. Do not edit already-applied baseline migrations except generated documentation snapshots if the repo convention requires it.
[ ] Replace HTTP route surfaces such as `/orgs/...` with tenant-named routes such as `/tenants/...`; remove legacy route registrations rather than keeping aliases.

[ ] Replace JSON fields and SDK/client request structs: `org`, `org_id`, `org_slug`, `organization`, and `owner_org` become `tenant`, `tenant_id`, or `tenant_slug` according to the resource being addressed.
[ ] Replace permission and token docs so service-token permissions say what the credential can do, and resource scopes say where it can do it.
[ ] Update tests to assert legacy fields/routes are gone, not merely deprecated.

[ ] Update README and API docs with a glossary matching OIDC/SaaS terminology: AuthKit instance/realm, tenant, native user, tenant membership, tenant role, tenant issuer, delegated user, service token.
[ ] Coordinate host repos after the hard cut: OpenRails, Doujins, Hentai0, Cozy Art, and Tensorhub must compile against tenant/service-token names only.

issues.name: OIDC tenant issuers and minimal delegated users
issues.description:
Add the dedicated delegated-identity model we settled on: tenants can trust external OIDC issuers, and AuthKit can record delegated/federated users as external principals without making them native users.

## Model

- `tenant_issuers`: tenant-owned trusted OIDC issuers. Stores `issuer` (`iss`), `jwks_uri`, allowed audiences, display label, enabled flag, and key-refresh metadata.
- `delegated_users`: minimal reference to an external principal asserted by a tenant issuer. Stores only `id`, `tenant_id`, `issuer`, `subject`, `created_at`, and `last_seen_at` unless a concrete product need adds more fields later.
- Uniqueness is `(tenant_id, issuer, subject)` because OIDC `sub` is only issuer-local.

## Non-goals

Do not store profile slop by default: no email, display name, avatar URL, external key, metadata JSON, or deleted_at unless a specific OpenRails/AuthKit flow needs it. Do not allow delegated users to register, log in with AuthKit-native credentials, reset passwords, or appear as native users.

## Delegation chain rule

AuthKit does not need to model every upstream delegation layer. If Cozy delegates `cozy/PaulFidika` to Tensorhub and Tensorhub delegates that onward to OpenRails, OpenRails still records a normal delegated user for its tenant: `(tenant_id=tensorhub, issuer=<registered issuer>, subject=cozy/PaulFidika)`. The chain is encoded in issuer/subject semantics, not extra AuthKit tables.

## Permissions on delegated JWTs

Delegated JWTs may carry tenant-scoped permissions for frontend/direct use by the relying service. Example: a Cozy Art user token can carry `self`; a Cozy Art admin token can carry membership/refund admin permissions; Tensorhub can delegate `cozy-art` with balance/self permissions to call Tensorhub OpenRails directly. These permissions are separate from service-token permissions.
tasks: [ ] Add numbered migrations for `tenant_issuers` and `delegated_users` with minimal fields, tenant FK, enabled/disabled issuer state, and unique `(tenant_id, issuer, subject)` on delegated users.
[ ] Keep the delegated user schema minimal: `id`, `tenant_id`, `issuer`, `subject`, `created_at`, `last_seen_at`; add no profile/email/avatar/metadata fields without a concrete product requirement.
[ ] Implement tenant issuer CRUD/reconcile APIs using OIDC terminology: `issuer`, `jwks_uri`, `audiences`, `kid`, `claims`, `enabled`.
[ ] Implement delegated-token verification against a tenant issuer: validate signature via JWKS, `iss`, `aud`, expiration/not-before, and required `sub`; reject missing or ambiguous issuer matches.
[ ] Implement get-or-touch delegated user resolution that creates the minimal delegated user row on first valid token use and updates `last_seen_at` on later use.
[ ] Keep delegated users out of native-user login, registration, password reset, email verification, and passkey flows.
[ ] Expose delegated identity to embedding hosts as `(tenant_id, issuer, subject, delegated_user_id)` and not as a native `user_id`.
[ ] Add tests for two issuers with the same `sub`, disabled issuer denial, wrong audience denial, stale JWKS/key rotation behavior, and delegated users not being native users.
[ ] Document how this maps to OIDC federation and how it differs from Keycloak-style user import/linking.
[ ] Define delegated JWT permission claim handling separately from service-token permissions; both may use permission strings, but they resolve to different principal types.
[ ] Add examples for end-user self permissions, tenant-admin permissions, and tenant-as-delegated-user permissions such as Tensorhub delegating `cozy-art`.

issues.name: Tenant manifest bootstrap for closed-registration deployments
issues.description:
Provide a generic DevOps bootstrap path for AuthKit instances that do not allow public tenant registration. This mirrors the deployment pattern we discussed for OpenRails: mount a manifest, reconcile declared tenants/issuers/roles/service tokens idempotently, and avoid modeling bootstrap authority as a fake platform/admin/operator tenant.

## Target behavior

An AuthKit instance can start with `AUTHKIT_TENANTS_FILE=/path/to/tenants.yaml`. The reconciler creates or updates declared tenants, tenant issuers, tenant roles, and optional service-token outputs. It should be safe to run at startup or as a one-shot job.

## Hard-cut policy

No `operator_org_slug`, no `platform tenant`, no `admin tenant`, and no special tenant that exists only to bootstrap other tenants. Bootstrap authority is deployment authority, not a domain entity inside AuthKit.
tasks: [ ] Define manifest schema v1 with `tenants[]`, each containing stable slug/id, display name, registration mode, issuers, roles, and optional service token outputs.
[ ] Implement idempotent manifest reconciliation behind a lock so multiple app replicas cannot race tenant/token creation.
[ ] Allow manifest-declared tenant issuers to set OIDC `issuer`, `jwks_uri`, allowed `audiences`, and enabled state.
[ ] Allow manifest-declared roles/permissions using tenant-role vocabulary, not org-role vocabulary.

[ ] Allow manifest-declared service tokens with arbitrary permissions, arbitrary resource scopes, and arbitrary output targets such as Vault KV path/field or local file. Do not hardcode OpenRails/Doujins permissions.
[ ] If an output already contains a non-empty token, preserve it; otherwise mint and write a new opaque service token. Include an explicit rotate workflow separately from startup reconciliation.
[ ] Expose the reconciler as both startup hook and CLI/job command so production can run it with a deploy identity rather than giving every API pod long-lived Vault write access.
[ ] Add tests for idempotency, multi-replica lock behavior, issuer update/disable, service token output preservation, and invalid manifest rejection.
[ ] Document closed-registration deployments and the distinction between deployment bootstrap authority and AuthKit tenants.

issues.name: Registration modes for native users and tenants
issues.description:
Make AuthKit's supported instance shapes explicit using standard SaaS/OIDC vocabulary, with native end-user registration and tenant registration controlled separately. Some deployments are B2C native-user systems with no tenants (Doujins/Hentai0). Some are B2B tenant systems with native users and tenant memberships (Tensorhub). Some are closed-registration relying-party systems with tenants and delegated users but no native users (OpenRails embedded AuthKit).

## Supported shapes

- Native-user app: native user registration open, tenant registration disabled, delegated users unused.
- B2B/SaaS app: native user registration open, tenant registration open or invite/admin-only, memberships/roles enabled.
- Relying-party/service app: native user registration closed, tenant registration manifest/admin-bootstrap-only, delegated users enabled.

## Route mounting contract

AuthKit is embedded, so the host application is also responsible for not mounting public routes it does not want to expose. Registration-mode checks are defense-in-depth and backend enforcement; route groups remain modular so a host can simply omit public native-user registration routes, tenant registration routes, passkeys, OIDC login, etc.

## Public registration closed

When native-user or tenant public registration is closed, creation must happen through admin/bootstrap/backend-controlled flows only: manifest bootstrap, admin APIs mounted only on trusted/internal surfaces, or direct host-side provisioning. No public endpoint should be able to create that resource.

The implementation should make these modes deliberate and documented rather than inferred from old org flags.

## Examples

- OpenRails embedded AuthKit: tenant registration is admin-bootstrap/manifest-only; native end-user registration is closed.
- Doujins/Hentai0 AuthKit: native end-user registration is open; tenant registration can be closed/disabled.
- Tensorhub AuthKit: native end-user registration is open; tenant registration may be open, invite-only, or admin-mediated depending on product policy.

## Tenant auto-creation and slug reservation

Do not auto-create a tenant when a native user registers by default. Native users and tenants are different entity types with different lifecycle, billing, membership, transfer, and deletion semantics. Hosts that want personal workspaces can opt into that later as an explicit product feature, not as hidden registration behavior.

Native-user-only hosts such as Doujins/Hentai0 should be able to disable tenant registration entirely; in that mode user registration creates only `users` rows and no `tenants`, `tenant_memberships`, `tenant_roles`, `tenant_issuers`, or `delegated_users` rows.

For B2B hosts such as Tensorhub, optionally support a shared reserved-slug namespace between users and tenants so `users.username` and `tenants.slug` cannot collide without requiring automatic tenant creation. This should be host/mode-controlled, not mandatory for deployments with tenant registration disabled.
tasks: [ ] Add explicit config for native end-user registration mode: open, invite/admin-only, admin-bootstrap-only, or closed.
[ ] Add explicit config for tenant registration mode: open, invite/admin-only, admin-bootstrap-only/manifest-only, or closed.

[ ] Enforce native-user and tenant registration modes separately; closing tenant registration must not imply closing native-user registration, and closing native-user registration must not imply closing tenant registration.

[ ] Keep route groups modular so embedded host applications can avoid mounting public native-user registration routes, public tenant registration routes, or any other public auth surface they do not want exposed.
[ ] Ensure public registration-disabled modes require admin/bootstrap/backend-controlled creation paths: manifest bootstrap, internal admin APIs, or host-side provisioning.
[ ] Ensure native-user-only deployments can have zero tenants without fake personal/org rows unless a separate issue intentionally introduces personal tenants.
[ ] Ensure closed-registration relying-party deployments can have zero native users and still accept delegated-user JWTs for registered tenant issuers.
[ ] Ensure B2B deployments support tenant memberships with one role per membership by default.
[ ] Replace old org registration flags/docs/tests with the new registration-mode vocabulary.
[ ] Add mode matrix tests for Doujins/Hentai0-style native app, Tensorhub-style B2B app, and OpenRails-style relying-party app, including host-not-mounted public route behavior.
[ ] Document which APIs are enabled/disabled by registration mode and which route groups hosts should omit for closed-registration deployments.
[ ] Do not auto-create tenants on native-user registration by default; if personal/team workspaces are ever desired, require an explicit host opt-in and tenant kind/product policy.
[ ] Support native-user-only deployments like Doujins/Hentai0 where tenant registration is disabled and user registration creates no tenant-related rows.
[ ] For B2B deployments like Tensorhub, optionally add a transactional shared reserved-slug namespace so `users.username` and `tenants.slug` cannot collide without creating fake tenants.
[ ] Document that shared slug reservation is host/mode-controlled and unnecessary when tenant registration is fully disabled.

issues.name: OIDC claims and service-token authorization contract
issues.description:
Standardize the AuthKit authorization contract around OIDC claims for delegated/browser JWTs and OAuth-style service tokens for server-to-server credentials.

## Delegated JWT contract

Delegated user tokens are JWTs issued by a tenant issuer. AuthKit validates OIDC-compatible claims: `iss`, `sub`, `aud`, `exp`, `nbf`, `iat`, and JOSE `kid`. Optional claims may be passed through only when explicitly whitelisted by the embedding host.

## Service-token contract

Service tokens are opaque, revocable credentials owned by a tenant. They carry permissions/scopes and resource scopes after resolution. They are not JWTs, not native user sessions, and not delegated users. This aligns with the industry separation between federated user identity and client-credentials/service-account authorization.

## Browser/admin delegated JWT vs server service token

Delegated JWTs are the credential for frontend/browser direct calls to a relying service such as OpenRails. They represent a tenant-scoped external principal with OIDC `iss` + `sub` and permissions, for example `tenant=cozy-art`, `delegated_user=PaulFidika`, `permissions=[self]`, or `tenant=tensorhub`, `delegated_user=cozy-art`, `permissions=[self, view-balance]`.

Service tokens are only for server-to-server communication. They belong to a tenant and resolve to a service principal, never to a native user or delegated user. They are used for backend entitlement reads, balance reserve/capture/release, bootstrap/deploy automation, and service integrations.
tasks: [ ] Rename or replace `ResolvedOrgAccessToken`-style contracts with `ResolvedServiceToken` / `ResolvedTenantServiceToken` using tenant, permissions/scopes, resources, key id, token id, expiry, and revocation metadata.
[ ] Keep the token string opaque; do not encode tenant, scopes, resources, or delegated identity into the token value.
[ ] Define canonical resource-scope shape with OIDC/OAuth-friendly names: `kind`, `id`, and optional `tenant_id` where needed by host semantics.
[ ] Ensure permission checks consistently distinguish identity (`iss`/`sub` or service token id), tenant boundary, permissions/scopes, and resource scopes.
[ ] Ensure delegated JWT resolution and service-token resolution return different principal types so hosts cannot accidentally treat service tokens as users or delegated users as native users.
[ ] Update middleware/contracts/docs to use `principal`, `native_user`, `delegated_user`, and `service_token` terms explicitly.
[ ] Add tests for principal-type separation and wrong-token-type denial on native-user-only, delegated-user, and service-token routes.
[ ] Update examples to show OpenRails validating Doujins/Hentai0 delegated JWTs via JWKS while using service tokens only for server-to-server entitlement reads.
[ ] Document that frontend/browser direct calls use delegated JWTs with OIDC `iss` + `sub` plus permissions, not service tokens.
[ ] Enforce that service tokens resolve only to service principals owned by a tenant; they must not impersonate native users or delegated users.
[ ] Add tests proving delegated JWTs can authorize browser/admin self/admin routes while service tokens authorize only server-to-server routes, with wrong-token-type denial both ways.

completed_features.name: Sign In With Solana (SIWS)
completed_features.summary: Implemented SIWS authentication with challenge/login/link endpoints, Ed25519 signature verification, and nonce-based replay protection.
